message.content:
Lighthouse’s **SEO category** has a small, fixed set of audits, and Google documents them on web.dev as a group rather than as separate pages for every audit ID. Google does **not** publish an official, always‑current, canonical list of all SEO audits in one place, and the set can change between Lighthouse versions.[8][9] Because of that, what follows is the **current, documented set of SEO audits on web.dev and Chrome for Developers**, plus their **known Lighthouse audit IDs** where Google exposes them in examples or code.

> Important limitation: Google’s official docs show the SEO audits primarily by *name and behavior*; the exact internal IDs (like `document-title`) are usually visible only in Lighthouse reports and the Lighthouse GitHub source, not in web.dev. Where an audit ID is not verifiable in official docs or code, I do **not** invent it; I only list IDs that are clearly used by Lighthouse in the open‑source repository.[7][8][9]

---

## 1. `document-title`

- **Audit ID:** `document-title`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks that the page has a **non‑empty `<title>` element** in the document `<head>`. A missing or empty title is an SEO issue because search engines use it as a primary ranking and display signal.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers “SEO audits” overview (lists “Document has a `<title>` element” as an SEO check):  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub shows the audit implemented as `document-title.js`, with ID `document-title`.[7]

---

## 2. `meta-description`

- **Audit ID:** `meta-description`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks that the page has a **`<meta name="description">` with a non‑empty `content`** attribute. A missing description can lead to worse snippets and click‑through in search results.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers “SEO audits” overview (lists “Document has a meta description”):  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub has `meta-description.js` with ID `meta-description`.[7]

---

## 3. `http-status-code`

- **Audit ID:** `http-status-code`  
- **What it verifies:** Ensures the final document is served with a **successful HTTP status code (2xx)**. Non‑successful codes (3xx, 4xx, 5xx) are flagged because they can prevent indexing or cause SEO problems.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers “SEO audits” overview (describes the SEO check “Page has successful HTTP status code”):  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Implemented as `http-status-code.js` with ID `http-status-code` in Lighthouse GitHub.[7]

---

## 4. `link-text`

- **Audit ID:** `link-text`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks that **links have descriptive text**, not just generic “click here” or empty/poorly labeled anchors. This helps both accessibility and SEO by giving context about the target.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers SEO docs (describes “Links have descriptive text” in SEO audits):  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse source includes `link-text.js` with ID `link-text`.[7]

---

## 5. `crawlable-anchors`

- **Audit ID:** `crawlable-anchors`  
- **What it verifies:** Ensures that **anchor elements (`<a>`) that look like links are crawlable** by search engines:  
  - They use `href` attributes with valid URLs (not only `onclick` handlers or JavaScript pseudo‑URLs).  
  - They are not disabled or malformed in ways that block crawling.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers SEO docs include “Links are crawlable” as an SEO audit:  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub uses the `crawlable-anchors` ID in the corresponding audit file.[7]

---

## 6. `is-crawlable`

- **Audit ID:** `is-crawlable`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks if the **page or its critical resources are blocked from crawling** by mechanisms such as `robots.txt` or `<meta name="robots">` directives that prevent indexing. If blocked, the page may not appear in search results.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers SEO docs reference “Page isn’t blocked from indexing” (crawlability checks):  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub exposes `is-crawlable.js` with ID `is-crawlable`.[7]

---

## 7. `robots-txt`

- **Audit ID:** `robots-txt`  
- **What it verifies:** Validates that the site’s **`/robots.txt` file is accessible and syntactically valid**, and that it isn’t inadvertently blocking the tested page (or all crawling).  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers SEO docs (robots.txt validation is one of the SEO checks listed):  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub includes the audit as `robots-txt.js` with ID `robots-txt`.[7]

---

## 8. `image-alt`

- **Audit ID:** `image-alt`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks that **images have `alt` attributes** (particularly meaningful content images). This is primarily an accessibility requirement but also helps image search and understanding of page content.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers SEO docs list “Images have `alt` attributes” as an SEO check:  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub includes `image-alt.js` with ID `image-alt`.[7]

---

## 9. `hreflang`

- **Audit ID:** `hreflang`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks correctness of **`rel="alternate" hreflang="…"` link annotations**, including:  
  - Proper language/region codes.  
  - Correct use of return links and canonical clusters.  
  - No obvious syntax errors that would make them unusable for internationalization.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers SEO docs include an audit for `hreflang` usage:  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub has the audit file `hreflang.js` with ID `hreflang`.[7]

---

## 10. `canonical`

- **Audit ID:** `canonical`  
- **What it verifies:** Validates **canonical URL signals**, typically checking that:  
  - There is at most one `<link rel="canonical">` per page.  
  - The canonical is not self‑contradictory, broken, or pointing to a non‑indexable URL.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - Chrome for Developers SEO docs mention canonicalization checks (“Document has a valid rel=canonical”):  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/seo/`[9]  
  - Implemented in Lighthouse as `canonical.js` with ID `canonical`.[7]

---

## 11. `font-size`

- **Audit ID:** `font-size`  
- **What it verifies:** Ensures that the **text on mobile is legible** by measuring font sizes and the proportion of text below a minimum recommended size. Poor legibility harms mobile usability and can indirectly affect search performance.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - web.dev article “Font size” in SEO context (part of “SEO audits” group):  
    `https://web.dev/font-size/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub includes `font-size.js` with ID `font-size`.[7]

---

## 12. `plugins`

- **Audit ID:** `plugins`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks that the page **does not rely on legacy browser plugins** (for example, Flash) that modern browsers and search engines do not support, which can lead to critical content being invisible to users and crawlers.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - web.dev article on “Plugins” under SEO audits:  
    `https://web.dev/plugins/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub contains `plugins.js` with ID `plugins`.[7]

---

## 13. `structured-data`

- **Audit ID:** `structured-data`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks for **valid structured data** on the page, using the Rich Results / schema validation logic embedded in Lighthouse. It reports:  
  - Presence of structured data items.  
  - Whether they produce **errors or warnings** according to the rich results validator.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - web.dev “Structured data” (SEO audit for Lighthouse):  
    `https://web.dev/structured-data/`[9]  
  - Implemented as `structured-data.js` with ID `structured-data` in Lighthouse GitHub.[7]

---

## 14. `viewport`

- **Audit ID:** `viewport`  
- **What it verifies:** Ensures the page has a **responsive `<meta name="viewport">` tag** configured correctly for mobile devices (for example, `width=device-width, initial-scale=1`). Missing or incorrect configuration affects mobile usability and mobile search signals.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - web.dev “Viewport” article in SEO audits group:  
    `https://web.dev/viewport/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub uses `viewport.js` with ID `viewport`.[7]

---

## 15. `tap-targets`

- **Audit ID:** `tap-targets`  
- **What it verifies:** Checks that **tap targets (links, buttons, controls)** are large enough and spaced sufficiently on mobile so that users can tap them accurately. This is a mobile UX and mobile‑SEO consideration.  
- **Official documentation URL:**  
  - web.dev “Tap targets” article under SEO audits:  
    `https://web.dev/tap-targets/`[9]  
  - Lighthouse GitHub contains `tap-targets.js` with ID `tap-targets`.[7]

---

## 16. `mobile-friendly` / mobile‑SEO grouping

Lighthouse historically grouped some of the above mobile‑focused audits (`viewport`, `font-size`, `tap-targets`) under a **“Mobile friendly”** or **“Mobile SEO”** section in the report, but those are labels, not separate audit IDs.[8][9] The underlying individual audits are the ones listed above.

---

## How complete is this list?

- Chrome for Developers’ **“SEO audits”** documentation explicitly lists the Lighthouse SEO checks and links out to individual web.dev articles for most of them.[8][9]  
- The Lighthouse **GitHub repository** is the authoritative place to confirm exact audit IDs (the filenames and `id` fields in `lighthouse-core/audits/seo/*`).[7]  
- Between these two official sources, the SEO category currently includes the **15 concrete audits** listed above. If Google adds or removes audits in future Lighthouse versions, the change will appear first in the GitHub repo and then in the Chrome/web.dev docs; there is no static, version‑independent canonical list guaranteed to stay the same.
